message.content: Adversaries may use proxies to direct network traffic between systems or act as intermediaries for communications with command and control (C2) servers, thereby avoiding direct connections to their infrastructure. This technique helps manage C2 communications, reduce simultaneous outbound connections, provide resiliency, and exploit trusted communication paths to avoid detection. Tools like HTRAN, ZXProxy, and ZXPortMap facilitate traffic redirection through proxies or port redirection. Adversaries may chain multiple proxies or use Content Delivery Networks (CDNs) to further obscure the source of malicious traffic. Notable examples include APT41's CLASSFON, Aria-body's reverse SOCKS proxy, and Dridex's backconnect module. Mitigation strategies include monitoring network traffic for unusual proxy usage, implementing strict network segmentation, and employing robust firewall rules to detect and block unauthorized proxy connections. Regularly updating threat intelligence and employing advanced intrusion detection systems can also help identify and mitigate proxy-based threats.
